title: Goyal Mg Gases Private Limited vs Hindustan Cables Limited & Ors on 21 February, 2023
body: Goyal Mg Gases Private Limited vs Hindustan Cables Limited & Ors on 21 February, 2023 Author: Yashwant Varma Bench: Yashwant Varma $~17 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + OMP (ENF.) (COMM.) 263/2018 & EX.APPL.(OS) 880/2019 (Direction), EX.APPL.(OS) 3668/2022 (Clarification in Respect Of Amount), I.A. 16806/2018(U.O. XXI R. 43 & 46 CPC r/w S.151 ), I.A. 305/2019(U.O. XXI R. 54 CPC r/w S. 151 ) GOYAL MG GASES PRIVATE LIMITED ..... Decree Holder Through: Mr. Tarun Singla and Mr. K.C. Joshi, Advs. versus HINDUSTAN CABLES LIMITED & ORS. ..... Judgement Debtors Through: Mr. G. Umapathy, Sr. Adv. with Mr. Rajesh Kajla and Mr. Aditya Singh, Advs. Ms. Shilpi Chowdhary, Adv. for JD-2. CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE YASHWANT VARMA ORDER % 21.02.2023 1. The solitary objection which remains for the consideration of the Court is whether the Respondent is liable to pay future interest in light of the operative directions as appearing in the award. Undisputedly, although the Section 34 challenge raised in respect of the award dated 07 November 2009 has come to be dismissed, an appeal under Section 37 is stated to be pending. However, no order of restraint is stated to operate thereon. 2. The objection raised at the behest of the Respondents essentially is that from the Claim Statement which was filed before the Arbitral Tribunal by the Enforcement Petitioner, it is evident that it had clearly and in unequivocal terms sought the award of interest @ 24%. Mr. Umapathy, learned Senior Counsel appearing for the Signature Not Verified Respondent, draws the attention of the Court to the operative Digitally Signed By:NEHA Signing Date:24.02.2023 18:29:32 directions as framed by the Arbitral Tribunal and which read thus:- "My decision on the points raised for adjudication is as under: Claim No.1 to 10 Rs.1,79,38,910/- (it includes an & 13 amount of Rs.70,00,000/- awarded in the interim Award dated 14th December, 2006 Claim No. 11 Rejected Claim No. 12 Rejected Claim No. 13 Already part of 1,79,38,910 as mentioned against Claim No.1 to 10 Claim No. 14 Rejected Claim No. 15 Rejected Claim No. 16 Rejected Claim No. 17 (a) Rejected (b) Rejected Claim No. 18 Interest @18% on the claim amount awarded of Rs.1,79,38,910 from the date it is due till 31.10.2009 as mentioned in Ann-F of the Claim Statement Claim No. 19 Rs.5,00,000 as arbitral expenses M/s Hindustan Cables & Anr. (Respondent) is called upon to make the payment within 90 (Ninety) days of pronouncement of the Award, failing which, interest @ 18% shall be applied to the entire amount remaining unpaid. In the event of failure to pay the execution of the Award can be filed. Appropriate stamp paper has been affixed and the award is signed on 7th November, 2009 in the presence of both the parties and a signed copy of the same is provided to each of the parties." 3. According to learned Senior Counsel, once the Arbitral Tribunal had failed to provide for the payment of future interest, no interest would be liable to be paid post the expiry of ninety days from the date of the Award. It was submitted that since the Arbitral Tribunal has specifically dealt with the liability of the Respondents to pay interest both for the past as well as pendente lite period but had refrained from awarding any interest for the future, it must be presumed that relief to the aforesaid extent stood refused and negated. Learned Senior Counsel also drew the attention of the Court to the judgment rendered by the Supreme Court in Coal Linker vs. Cola Signature Not Verified Digitally Signed By:NEHA Signing Date:24.02.2023 18:29:32 India Limited1 which while dealing with the question of payment of future interest, had observed and held as under:- "16. Admittedly, in the instant case interest has been granted by the arbitrator in the award for the first two periods. But interest has not been granted by the arbitrator in the award for the last period. As noted above, the appellant awardee herein, filed an application under Section 17 of the Act for pronouncing a judgment in terms of the award. So there is no scope for the executing court to go beyond the award and grant interest for the post-award period which was not granted in the award. Here the executing court has gone beyond the award and thus had gone beyond its jurisdiction and passed a decree which thus becomes a nullity." In view of the aforesaid, it was contended that the claim as made by the Enforcement Petitioner cannot sustain. 4. Learned counsel appearing for the Enforcement Petitioner, on the other hand, submits that the concept of a deemed refusal firstly does not arise under the provisions of the Act as it stands today especially in light of Section 31(7)(b) . It was his submission that in any case that it would be incorrect to read the Award as being totally silent on this aspect since the sole arbitrator had clearly provided that the Respondents would make payments within ninety days of pronouncement of the Award failing which interest @ 18% shall be applied to the entire amount remaining to be paid. According to learned counsel, this is a clear and categorical expression of the intent of the Arbitral Tribunal to award interest even for the future. 5. Insofar as the question of silence and a deemed refusal of payment of interest is concerned that would clearly merit evaluation bearing in mind Section 31(7)(b) as it stands today. As is manifest from a reading of clause (b), the claimant is entitled to interest for the future periods unless the Arbitral Tribunal directs otherwise. This aspect was examined by the Court in a recent decision rendered in M/S. Associated Construction CO vs. Union of India2 where the 1 (2009) 9 SCC 491 Signature Not Verified 2 Digitally Signed Order dated 15.02.2023 in OMP (ENF.) (COMM.) 122/2021 By:NEHA Signing Date:24.02.2023 18:29:32 following principles were enunciated: - "13. From the aforesaid recordal of facts it is manifest that the Tribunal does not appear to have principally ruled out the payment of interest post the award having been rendered. The directions framed in paragraph 226 of the award would also have to be read in conjunction with the findings that the Tribunal chose to return while dealing with the claims individually. Undisputedly, while recording its conclusions with respect to Claim Nos. 2 and 3 the Tribunal had awarded interest @ 12% p.a. While the Tribunal does use the phrase "delayed period" while recording its conclusions in respect of those claims, it fails to, at least categorically, proceed to refuse interest for the period post the pronouncement of award. ........... 16. The submission that the Arbitral Tribunal having refused to specifically award interest in respect of Claim Nos. 2 and 3 and thus the Execution Petitioner being disentitled for the same, in any case, clearly flies in the face of Section 31(7)(b) . As was noticed hereinabove, clause (b) of Section 31(7) mandates that any sum which is directed to be paid by and in terms of an arbitral award would carry interest unless the award otherwise directs. The expression "otherwise directs" would, therefore, clearly be liable to be construed as referring to a specific refusal to award interest for the future period. A mere or perceived silence on the part of the Arbitral Tribunal to deal with the issue of future interest cannot be understood as being a refusal of that relief to the claimant. In fact, it is such a situation which is ultimately taken care of by Section 31(7)(b) . In fact, the said provision mandatorily commands the payment of interest for the period between the date of the award and when the amounts may be ultimately paid unless there be a direction of the Tribunal to the contrary. The interest under Section 31(7)(b) is thus a component which becomes payable by operation of law except where the Tribunal may have specifically recorded that interest for the future would not be liable to be paid. The Court thus comes to the firm conclusion that unless there be a specific recordal of reasons for refusal of future interest in the Arbitral Award, the issue would clearly and unmistakably be governed by Section 31(7)(b) and consequently the claimant before the Arbitral Tribunal would be entitled to claim interest for the future period." 6. However, it becomes pertinent to note that clause (b) as it stands in its present form came to be introduced in the statute book only by virtue of Act 3 of 2016 which was ordained to come into effect retrospectively from 23 October 2015. It could therefore possibly have been argued that the amended Section 31(7)(b) would have no application to an Award which was rendered in 2009. The Signature Not Verified Digitally Signed By:NEHA Signing Date:24.02.2023 18:29:32 Court however notes that the aforesaid issue need not be dealt with or ruled upon in light of what follows hereinafter. 7. The Court finds that the operative portion of the Award clearly contemplates and makes a provision for the payment of interest upon a failure of the Respondent to deposit the entire amounts determined as payable to the claimant within ninety days of pronouncement of the Award. There is in fact no ambiguity in the Award which clearly places the Respondent under an obligation to pay interest upon the expiry of ninety days. The Enforcement Petitioner, thus, clearly appears to be entitled to be paid interest @ 18% p.a. post the expiry of ninety days from 07 November 2009 till the remainder amount payable under the Award along with up-to-date interest is deposited and made over to the Enforcement Petitioner. 8. The objections consequently stand negatived in the above terms. The Respondents to proceed in light of the aforesaid directions. 9. Let the matter be called again on 17.05.2023 for reviewing progress. YASHWANT VARMA, J. FEBRUARY 21, 2023/bh Signature Not Verified Digitally Signed By:NEHA Signing Date:24.02.2023 18:29:32
